definitions: Used as a greeting or to begin a phone conversation
An utterance of hello; a greeting
examples: She gave a cheerful hello to everyone
hello.etymology: From Old English 'hāl', meaning healthy or whole

definitions: The earth, together with all of its countries, peoples, and natural features
A particular region or group of countries
Human and social interaction
world.etymology: From Old English 'woruld', meaning human existence

definitions: A process or set of rules to be followed in calculations or other problem-solving operations, especially by a computer
examples: The algorithm sorts the data in linear time
algorithm.etymology: From the name of Persian mathematician al-Khwarizmi

definitions: A large heavy-bodied nonvenomous snake
A high-level general-purpose programming language
examples: The python coiled around the branch
Python is widely used in data science

definitions: A book or electronic resource that lists the words of a language and gives their meaning
A reference book on a particular subject
examples: I looked up the word in the dictionary
dictionary.etymology: From Medieval Latin 'dictionarium', from Latin 'dictio' meaning word

definitions: To try to find something by looking carefully
An act of searching for something
examples: A thorough search of the area
search.etymology: From Old French 'cerchier', from Latin 'circare' meaning to go around

definitions: A machine with moving parts that converts power into motion
A thing that is the agent or instrument of a particular process
examples: The car has a powerful engine
The search engine returns results instantly
engine.etymology: From Old French 'engin', from Latin 'ingenium' meaning talent

definitions: A tree-like data structure used for efficient retrieval of keys in a dataset of strings, also called a prefix tree
examples: The autocomplete feature uses a trie for fast prefix matching
trie.etymology: From 'retrieval', coined by Edward Fredkin in 1960

definitions: A flower, especially one cultivated for its beauty
To produce flowers; to be in flower
A state or period of greatest beauty or productivity
A collection of items of the same type stored in a hidden place
A high-speed storage mechanism for frequently accessed data
To store data in a cache
examples: The browser cache speeds up page loading
He cached the supplies in the cave

definitions: The branch of science concerned with the nature and properties of matter and energy
physics.etymology: From Latin 'physica', from Greek 'phusike' meaning natural science

definitions: Vocal or instrumental sounds combined in such a way as to produce beauty of form, harmony, and expression of emotion
examples: She has a talent for music
music.etymology: From Old French 'musique', from Latin 'musica', from Greek 'mousike'

definitions: Pleasing the senses or mind aesthetically
Of a very high standard; excellent
examples: A beautiful sunset over the ocean
beautiful.etymology: From Middle English 'beautefull', from 'beaute' meaning beauty

definitions: Facts, information, and skills acquired through experience or education
The theoretical or practical understanding of a subject
examples: His knowledge of history is impressive
knowledge.etymology: From Middle English 'knowleche', from 'knowen' meaning to know

definitions: The body of words used in a particular language
The body of words known to an individual person
A list of difficult or unfamiliar words with an explanation of their meanings
examples: Expand your vocabulary by reading widely
vocabulary.etymology: From Medieval Latin 'vocabularium', from Latin 'vocabulum' meaning word

definitions: The occurrence and development of events by chance in a happy or beneficial way
Good fortune; lucky discoveries
serendipity.etymology: Coined by Horace Walpole in 1754, from the fairy tale 'The Three Princes of Serendip'

definitions: Lasting for a very short time
Having a very short life cycle
examples: The ephemeral beauty of cherry blossoms
ephemeral.etymology: From Greek 'ephemeros', meaning lasting only a day

definitions: Fluent or persuasive in speaking or writing
Clearly expressing or indicating something
examples: An eloquent speech moved the audience
Her silence was more eloquent than words
eloquent.etymology: From Latin 'eloquentem', from 'eloqui' meaning to speak out

definitions: The capacity to recover quickly from difficulties; toughness
The ability of a substance to spring back into shape; elasticity
examples: The resilience of the human spirit
resilience.etymology: From Latin 'resilire', meaning to leap back

definitions: Present, appearing, or found everywhere
Existing or being everywhere at the same time
